category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Linear Algebra — Matrix Representation of Linear Maps

name.text: Linear Maps - Matrix of T: R^2 to R^2

questiontext.text: Let \(T:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2\) be defined by \(T(x,y)=(2x+y,\,x-3y)\). The matrix of \(T\) with respect to the standard basis is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\1&-3\end{pmatrix}\)

Column \(j\) of the matrix = \(T(e_j)\). \(T(e_1)=T(1,0)=(2,1)\), \(T(e_2)=T(0,1)=(1,-3)\). So the matrix is \(\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\1&-3\end{pmatrix}\).

questiontext.text: Let \(D:P_2\to P_1\) be the differentiation map \(D(p)=p'\). With basis \(\{1,x,x^2\}\) for \(P_2\) and \(\{1,x\}\) for \(P_1\), the matrix of \(D\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\begin{pmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&2\end{pmatrix}\)

\(D(1)=0=0\cdot1+0\cdot x\), \(D(x)=1=1\cdot1+0\cdot x\), \(D(x^2)=2x=0\cdot1+2\cdot x\). Columns are the coordinates of images: \([0,0]^T,[1,0]^T,[0,2]^T\). Matrix: \(\begin{pmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&2\end{pmatrix}\).

name.text: Linear Maps - Integration T: P_2 to P_3

questiontext.text: Let \(T:P_2\to P_3\) be defined by \(T(p)(x)=\displaystyle\int_0^x p(t)\,dt\). With bases \(\{1,t,t^2\}\) for \(P_2\) and \(\{1,x,x^2,x^3\}\) for \(P_3\), the matrix of \(T\) is:
If \([T]_B\) is the matrix of \(T\) in basis \(B\) and \(P\) is the change-of-basis matrix from \(B\) to \(B'\), then the matrix in basis \(B'\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(P^{-1}[T]_B P\)

Change of basis: \([T]_{B'}=P^{-1}[T]_B P\), where columns of \(P\) are the old basis vectors expressed in the new basis. Two matrices related this way are called similar. Similar matrices represent the same linear map in different bases and have the same eigenvalues, trace, and determinant.

questiontext.text: The matrix of the rotation by angle \(\theta\) (counterclockwise) in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) with respect to the standard basis is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta&-\sin\theta\\\sin\theta&\cos\theta\end{pmatrix}\)

\(R_\theta(e_1)=(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)\), \(R_\theta(e_2)=(-\sin\theta,\cos\theta)\). Column 1 = \((\cos\theta,\sin\theta)^T\), Column 2 = \((-\sin\theta,\cos\theta)^T\). This is an orthogonal matrix with \(\det=1\).

name.text: Linear Maps - Projection onto a Line

questiontext.text: The matrix of the orthogonal projection onto the line \(y=x\) in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\dfrac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&1\end{pmatrix}\)

Projection onto \(u=(1,1)/\sqrt{2}\): \(P=uu^T=\frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&1\end{pmatrix}\). Check: \(P(1,1)^T=(1,1)^T\) ✓, \(P(1,-1)^T=(0,0)^T\) ✓. Note \(P^2=P\) (idempotent) and \(P^T=P\) (symmetric).

questiontext.text: If \(T:\mathbb{R}^5\to\mathbb{R}^3\) is a linear map with rank 3, then \(\dim(\ker T)=\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 2

Rank–Nullity theorem: \(\dim(\ker T)+\text{rank}(T)=\dim(\text{domain})=5\). With rank 3: \(\dim(\ker T)=5-3=2\).

questiontext.text: The matrix of reflection about the \(x\)-axis in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}\)

Reflection about \(x\)-axis: \((x,y)\mapsto(x,-y)\). So \(T(e_1)=e_1\), \(T(e_2)=-e_2\). Matrix: \(\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}\). This is symmetric and orthogonal with determinant \(-1\).

questiontext.text: If \([S]_B=A\) and \([T]_B=B\), then the matrix of \(T\circ S\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(BA\)

Matrix multiplication corresponds to composition in the order \([T\circ S]=[ T][S]=BA\). Note the reversed order: apply \(S\) first (matrix \(A\)), then \(T\) (matrix \(B\)), giving \(BA\). This is why matrix multiplication is generally non-commutative.

questiontext.text: For a linear map \(T:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2\) with matrix \(\begin{pmatrix}3&1\\2&4\end{pmatrix}\), the trace and determinant are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) trace\(=7\), \(\det=10\)

trace\(=3+4=7\), \(\det=3\cdot4-1\cdot2=12-2=10\). The characteristic polynomial is \(\lambda^2-7\lambda+10=(\lambda-2)(\lambda-5)\), giving eigenvalues 2 and 5.

name.text: Linear Maps - Kernel of Derivative Map

questiontext.text: The kernel of the differentiation map \(D:C^\infty(\mathbb{R})\to C^\infty(\mathbb{R})\), \(D(f)=f'\), is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) The set of constant functions

\(\ker D=\{f:f'=0\}=\{\text{constant functions}\}\), a 1-dimensional subspace. The range is all of \(C^\infty(\mathbb{R})\) (since every smooth function has a smooth antiderivative, though not unique). The rank–nullity theorem does not apply in infinite dimensions in the usual sense.

questiontext.text: A linear map \(T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^3\) satisfies \(T^2=0\) (nilpotent). The maximum possible rank of \(T\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 1

Wait — actually if \(T^2=0\), then range\((T)\subseteq\ker(T)\). By rank–nullity: \(\text{rank}+\text{nullity}=3\). If rank\((T)=r\), then range\((T)\subseteq\ker(T)\), so \(\text{nullity}\geq r\), giving \(r+r\leq3\), i.e., \(r\leq3/2\), so \(r\leq1\). Maximum rank is 1 (e.g., \(T\) maps \(e_1\mapsto e_2, e_2\mapsto0, e_3\mapsto0\)).

questiontext.text: A linear map \(T:V\to V\) (where \(\dim V=n<\infty\)) is invertible if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\det[T]\neq0\)

In finite dimensions, the following are equivalent: (i) \(T\) is invertible, (ii) \(T\) is injective, (iii) \(T\) is surjective, (iv) \(\ker T=\{0\}\), (v) \(\det[T]\neq0\), (vi) 0 is not an eigenvalue of \(T\). These equivalences rely on the rank–nullity theorem and finite-dimensionality.

name.text: Linear Maps - Image and Kernel Complement

questiontext.text: If \(T:V\to W\) and \(S:V\to W\) are linear with the same matrix, then \(T\) and \(S\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Are the same map

Given the same basis, the same matrix uniquely determines the linear map. If \([T]_B=[S]_B\), then \(T(v)=S(v)\) for all \(v\in V\). A linear map is completely determined by its action on a basis, and the matrix encodes exactly this information.

answer.text: Are the same map (given the same bases)
May differ on a dense subset
Have the same kernel but may differ elsewhere
Are related by \(T=S+c\) for some constant

questiontext.text: For matrices \(A\) (\(m\times n\)) and \(B\) (\(n\times p\)), we have:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\text{rank}(AB)\leq\min(\text{rank}(A),\text{rank}(B))\)

The column space of \(AB\) is contained in the column space of \(A\), so rank\((AB)\leq\) rank\((A)\). Also rank\((AB)\leq\) rank\((B)\) (the row space of \(AB\) is contained in the row space of \(B\)). The Sylvester inequality also gives rank\((AB)\geq\) rank\((A)+\text{rank}(B)-n\).

questiontext.text: If \(T:V\to W\) is a linear map with matrix \(A\) (w.r.t. given bases), then the matrix of the dual (transpose) map \(T^*:W^*\to V^*\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(A^T\)

The dual map \(T^*(f)=f\circ T\) for \(f\in W^*\). With respect to dual bases, the matrix of \(T^*\) is the transpose \(A^T\). This explains why \(\text{rank}(A)=\text{rank}(A^T)\) — the column rank equals the row rank.

name.text: Linear Maps - Surjective T: R^3 to R^2

questiontext.text: A surjective linear map \(T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2\) has \(\dim(\ker T)\) equal to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 1

Surjective \(\Rightarrow\) rank\((T)=\dim(\mathbb{R}^2)=2\). By rank–nullity: nullity\(=3-2=1\). There is a 1-dimensional solution space (a line) for each output value, showing the "fibers" of \(T\) are parallel lines in \(\mathbb{R}^3\).

questiontext.text: Two \(n\times n\) matrices \(A\) and \(B\) are similar (\(B=P^{-1}AP\)) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) They represent the same linear map in different bases

Similarity captures the change-of-basis relationship: \(A\) and \(B=P^{-1}AP\) represent the same linear transformation \(T\) with respect to different bases (the columns of \(P\) being the new basis in terms of the old). They share eigenvalues, trace, determinant, rank, and characteristic polynomial.

answer.text: They represent the same linear map in different bases

name.text: Linear Maps - T: P_3 to P_3 via evaluation

questiontext.text: Let \(T:P_3\to\mathbb{R}^4\) be the evaluation map \(T(p)=(p(0),p(1),p(2),p(3))\). Then \(T\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) An isomorphism (bijective linear map)

A polynomial of degree \(\leq3\) is uniquely determined by its values at 4 distinct points (by Lagrange interpolation). So \(T\) is injective. Since \(\dim P_3=4=\dim\mathbb{R}^4\), \(T\) is also surjective, hence an isomorphism.

name.text: Linear Maps - CSIR Style: Matrix of T(x,y,z)

questiontext.text: Let \(T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^3\) be defined by \(T(x,y,z)=(x+y,\,y+z,\,x+z)\). The determinant of the matrix of \(T\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 2

Matrix: \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1&0\\0&1&1\\1&0&1\end{pmatrix}\). \(\det A=1(1-0)-1(0-1)+0=1+1=2\). Since \(\det\neq0\), \(T\) is invertible.
